title: Fitting serpentine springs to a sofa seat frame
subtitle: Replace or install no-sag sinuous wire springs across a sofa seat rail
excerpt: How to measure, cut, and fit sinuous (serpentine or no-sag) wire springs from front to back rail of a sofa seat frame, space them correctly across the width of the frame, clip them to the seat rails, and tie them with spring twine to prevent lateral spread.
sourceNotes: The Complete Guide to Upholstery, Cherry Dobson. The Upholstery Workshop, Frank Ahlen. FIRA spring specification guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A zig-zag formed wire spring, typically 9 or 10 gauge, that runs in a continuous wave from front to back rail in a furniture seat or back frame. Also called a serpentine spring or no-sag spring. The spring is cut to length and clipped into metal fixing clips nailed to the inside face of the rail. Lighter and faster to fit than coil springs, sinuous springs are the standard in most modern sofas.

A bent-metal clip designed to receive the hooked end of a sinuous spring wire and secure it to the timber seat rail. The clip is nailed to the rail at the correct depth before the spring is inserted. Two types: rigid clips (fixed) and tilting clips (allow adjustment of spring tension by angling the spring end up or down within the clip).

A strong jute or nylon twine laced across the top of the fitted sinuous springs to prevent the springs spreading laterally under load. Typically tied at 150 to 200 mm intervals from side to side, crossing each spring at its apex. Without twine, sinuous springs can splay outward and distort the seat profile under repeated use.

content.text: Replacing
sinuous springs

restores the support of a sofa that has become uncomfortable but otherwise has a sound frame and cover. The work requires removing the dust cover and seat fabric to access the spring rails, then cutting new springs to the same length as the originals. Match the gauge of the new springs to the old: 9 gauge is firm, 10 gauge is medium, 11 gauge is soft.
Step 1, remove the seat platform and old springs
Turn the sofa on its back and remove the dust cover using the staple remover. Remove the seat cushion platform fabric. Lift the old spring twine and cut it away. Lift each sinuous spring out of its
spring clips
: most hook out by simply lifting the spring end out of the clip. Count the number of springs and measure the length of one as a template for cutting new stock.
Step 2, nail the new spring clips
If the old
clips

are intact, reuse them. If any are pulled out or bent, replace with matching 9-gauge sinuous spring clips. Space the new spring positions evenly across the front rail, with the outermost springs 50 mm from each end of the rail and the remaining springs equally spaced: typically every 80 to 100 mm on a standard 3-seat sofa. Mark positions on both front and back rails and nail the clips into the timber face.
Step 3, cut and fit the springs

Cut each spring from stock using heavy-duty wire cutters. The correct length allows the spring to be seated in both clips with a slight forward bow: approximately 15 to 20 mm higher at the centre than at each end. Hook the front end of the spring down into the front clip, then lever the back end down into the back clip. The spring should require some effort to compress into the back clip: if it drops in easily, cut the next spring slightly longer.
Step 4, lace the springs with twine

Staple or knot spring twine to the side rail of the seat frame. Run the twine across all the springs at right angles to them (side to side), looping around each spring at its apex. Tension the twine so the springs are gently restrained but not pulled out of their natural bow. Tie off at the opposite rail and staple. Apply three or four rows of
spring twine
spaced evenly from front to back.
Step 5, re-cover and test

Refit the seat platform fabric over the springs, stapling to the rails. Refit the dust cover to the underside of the frame. Stand the sofa upright and sit on it to confirm the spring support is even across the width. An area of different firmness indicates a spring is not in its clips or is the wrong length.
Variations

Some sofas use Pirelli rubber webbing rather than sinuous springs. Rubber webbing is simpler to fit: cut to length and staple into flat brackets on the inside of the rail: but has a shorter service life and sags more over time. Pirelli webbing replacement is covered in a separate entry. Traditional coil springs require a separate entry covering spring tying methods, which are more complex than sinuous spring fitting.

attrs.symptom: One spring makes a clicking sound under load
attrs.cause: Spring end not fully seated in the clip; clip loose or pulling from the rail.
attrs.fix: Remove the spring, check the clip is firmly nailed to the rail, and refit the spring end fully into the clip. The wire should be fully seated with no tendency to spring out.

attrs.symptom: Springs spread sideways under use, giving an uneven seat profile
attrs.cause: Spring twine not fitted or too loose; twine has broken.
attrs.fix: Re-lace spring twine across all springs at three or four rows from front to back. Ensure each length of twine loops securely around each spring apex.

attrs.symptom: Seat feels harder than the original after new springs are fitted
attrs.cause: Higher-gauge (stiffer) spring stock used than the original.
attrs.fix: Replace with the correct gauge. If unsure, compare the coil diameter and wire gauge of the removed springs to the new stock before fitting.
